  3. Appointing authorities are encouraged but not required to reappoint Commissioners who have demonstrated professional competence and independence during their initial terms;

  4. Commissioners who have served two full terms (12 years) are ineligible for any further appointments to the Commission.

(3) Service Until Successor Qualified

  1. Commissioners whose terms expire continue to serve until their successors are appointed and qualified, ensuring continuity of Commission operations;

  2. This continuation may not exceed one year beyond term expiration;

  3. If a successor has not been appointed within one year of term expiration, the Commissioner position becomes vacant and the Commission operates with reduced membership until a successor is appointed;

  4. Commissioners serving beyond term expiration retain full voting rights and all powers of office until succession occurs.

(4) Vacancy Filling

  1. If a Commissioner position becomes vacant before term expiration due to death, resignation, removal, or other cause:
  2. The appointing authority that made the original appointment shall appoint a successor within 90 days of the vacancy;
  3. The successor serves the remainder of the unexpired term;
  4. If the remainder of the unexpired term is less than three years, the successor may be reappointed for one full six-year term, for a potential maximum of nine years total service;

  5. If the remainder of the unexpired term is three years or more, it counts as a full term for reappointment purposes, and the successor may be reappointed for one additional six-year term;

  6. If an appointing authority fails to fill a vacancy within 180 days, the Commission continues operations with reduced membership;

  7. The Commission may issue Determinations with reduced membership provided at least five Commissioners remain.

(5) Continuity During Political Transitions

  1. Commissioner appointments are not affected by changes in the appointing authority (e.g., new President, new House Speaker, new Senate Majority Leader);

  2. Appointing authorities may not remove Commissioners appointed by their predecessors;

  3. When filling vacancies or making reappointments, new appointing authorities should give strong consideration to continuity and institutional knowledge;

  4. This structure ensures the Commission maintains independence and expertise across political transitions.

docs.title: (f) Removal Procedures
docs.text:
(1) Grounds for Removal

Commissioners may be removed from office only for cause, which shall be limited to:

  1. Misconduct in office -- Including but not limited to:
  2. Acceptance of bribes or other corrupt payments;
  3. Use of Commission position for personal financial gain;
  4. Knowing dissemination of false information in official capacity;
  5. Violation of confidentiality requirements for closed deliberations;

  6. Material violation of conflict of interest or recusal requirements;

  7. Persistent neglect of duties -- Including but not limited to:

  8. Failure to attend three consecutive scheduled Commission meetings without excuse;
  9. Refusal to participate in Commission responsibilities over an extended period;

  10. Failure to maintain required expertise or professional competence;

  11. Incapacity -- Incapacity to perform the duties of office due to:

  12. Mental or physical impairment lasting more than six consecutive months;

  13. Incapacity that prevents meaningful participation in Commission functions;

  14. Conviction of serious crimes -- Conviction of:

  15. Any felony offense;
  16. Any misdemeanor crime involving fraud, dishonesty, or moral turpitude.

(2) Removal Process

  1. Initiation:
  2. Any Commissioner may introduce a motion for removal of another Commissioner for cause;
  3. The motion must be submitted in writing, specifying the grounds for removal and supporting evidence;

  4. The Commissioner subject to removal proceedings shall receive the motion and have 30 days to respond in writing;

  5. Investigation:

  6. The Commission may appoint an independent investigator to examine the removal allegations;
  7. The investigator shall have access to all relevant Commission records and may interview Commission staff and external witnesses;

  8. The investigator shall issue a written report within 60 days of appointment;

  9. Hearing:

  10. The Commission shall hold a hearing on the removal motion;
  11. The Commissioner subject to removal has the right to appear, present evidence, call witnesses, and be represented by counsel;
  12. The hearing may be closed to the public if it involves classified information, personnel matters, or matters that would violate privacy rights;

  13. A transcript shall be made of all hearing proceedings;

  14. Vote:

  15. Following the hearing, the Commission shall vote on whether to remove the Commissioner;
  16. Removal requires a supermajority vote of six of the nine Commissioners;
  17. The Commissioner subject to removal may not vote on their own removal;
  18. If the Commissioner subject to removal is recused, removal requires six votes of the remaining eight Commissioners;

  19. The vote shall be conducted by roll call and made part of the public record;

  20. Suspension Pending Removal:

  21. By supermajority vote (six of nine Commissioners), the Commission may suspend a Commissioner from duties pending final determination of removal proceedings;
  22. Suspension shall be without loss of salary or benefits;
  23. Suspended Commissioners do not count toward quorum and may not participate in deliberations or votes during suspension;
  24. Suspension may not exceed 180 days; if removal vote has not occurred within 180 days, suspension automatically terminates.

(3) Judicial Review

  1. Right to Appeal:
  2. Any Commissioner removed from office may appeal the removal to the United States District Court for the District of Columbia within 30 days of removal;

  3. The appeal shall be expedited and shall be heard by a three-judge panel;

  4. Standard of Review:

  5. The court shall review whether removal was for cause as defined in this section;
  6. The court shall review Commission procedures for compliance with this section;

  7. The court shall give deference to Commission factual findings but shall review legal determinations de novo;

  8. Remedies:

  9. If the court finds removal was not for cause or procedures were not followed, the court shall reinstate the Commissioner and may award back pay and benefits;
  10. If the court upholds removal, the decision is subject to appeal to the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit and subsequently to the Supreme Court.

(4) Protection from Political Removal

  1. Commissioners may not be removed based on:
  2. Disagreement with specific Determinations issued by the Commission;
  3. Political party affiliation or political views;
  4. Criticism of Congress or congressional operations in official capacity;
  5. Publication of research or testimony regarding congressional capacity;

  6. Any reason other than cause as defined in subsection (f)(1);

  7. Any attempted removal for political reasons shall be void ab initio and subject to immediate judicial invalidation;

  8. The appointing authority that appointed a Commissioner has no special authority to remove that Commissioner; all removals must be by Commission supermajority vote as specified above.

docs.title: (g) Chair Selection and Duties
docs.text:
(1) Election of Chair

  1. At the first meeting of each calendar year, the Commissioners shall elect from among their members a Chair of the Commission by majority vote;

  2. The Chair serves a two-year term in that role and may be reelected to subsequent two-year terms without limit;

  3. Service as Chair does not extend a Commissioner's term; Chair service is an additional duty during the Commissioner's regular term;

  4. If the Chair position becomes vacant during a two-year term due to term expiration, resignation, removal from Chair position, or other cause, the Commission shall elect a new Chair at its next scheduled meeting to serve the remainder of the two-year term.

(2) Chair Duties and Authorities

The Chair shall:

  1. Preside over Commission meetings -- Establish agenda, recognize speakers, manage debate, call votes, maintain order, and enforce procedures;

  2. Represent the Commission -- Serve as official spokesperson, testify before Congress, conduct media relations, and represent the Commission at official functions;

  3. Coordinate Commission activities -- Ensure timely completion of studies, adherence to publication schedules, implementation of Determination procedures, and fulfillment of reporting obligations;

  4. Supervise Executive Director -- Provide direction to the Executive Director regarding Commission priorities, work plans, and administrative matters (subject to overall Commission policy direction);

  5. Execute Commission decisions -- Sign official Determinations, transmit documents to Congress, and implement actions authorized by Commission vote;

  6. Emergency authority -- Make operational decisions requiring immediate action between scheduled Commission meetings, subject to ratification at the next meeting.

(3) Chair as First Among Equals

  1. The Chair has no additional vote beyond their vote as a Commissioner; all Commissioners have equal voting power;

  2. The Chair may not unilaterally block or advance any matter; all substantive decisions require Commission votes pursuant to voting procedures in Section 102;

  3. The Chair serves at the pleasure of the Commission; Commissioners may remove the Chair from that position (while retaining Commissioner status) by majority vote at any time;

  4. In the Chair's absence, the Commissioners present shall elect an Acting Chair from among themselves by majority vote to preside over that meeting.

(4) Vice Chair

The Commission may elect a Vice Chair to serve in the Chair's absence and to perform such other duties as the Commission may assign. The Vice Chair is elected by majority vote and serves at the pleasure of the Commission.

docs.title: (h) Compensation
docs.text:
(1) Commission Member Compensation

  1. Commissioners, other than the Chair, shall receive annual compensation at the rate established for Level III of the Executive Schedule under 5 U.S.C. Ã‚Â§ 5314, as adjusted from time to time;

  2. This compensation level reflects the senior professional responsibility and expertise required for Commission service and is comparable to other high-level independent agency positions.

(2) Chair Compensation

The Chair shall receive annual compensation at the rate established for Level II of the Executive Schedule under 5 U.S.C. Ã‚Â§ 5313, as adjusted from time to time, reflecting the additional responsibilities of leading the Commission and representing it publicly.

(3) Travel and Expenses

Commissioners shall be entitled to travel expenses, including per diem in lieu of subsistence, in accordance with sections 5702 and 5703 of title 5, United States Code, when traveling on official Commission business.

(4) Benefits

Commissioners are federal employees for purposes of federal employee benefits including health insurance, life insurance, and retirement benefits under the Federal Employees Retirement System (FERS) or the Civil Service Retirement System (CSRS), as applicable. Years of service as a Commissioner count toward federal retirement vesting and benefit calculations.

(5) No Additional Compensation

Commissioners may not receive any compensation from the federal government beyond the compensation specified in this subsection, other than permitted outside employment or consulting that does not conflict with Commission duties.

docs.title: Section 102. Commission Powers, Duties, and Operations
(a) General Authority
docs.text:
The Commission shall have the following authorities necessary to fulfill its mission:

(1) Study and Assessment Authority

The Commission may conduct comprehensive studies and assessments of:

  1. Congressional resource adequacy across all categories addressed in this Act;
  2. Member compensation levels, staff compensation levels, technology infrastructure, security resources, office operational costs, and member support systems;
  3. Comparable positions in federal government, state governments, other democracies, and private sector organizations;
  4. Recruitment and retention challenges facing Congress;
  5. Operational costs and resource requirements for effective legislative operations;
  6. Any other matters related to congressional institutional capacity.

(2) Investigative Powers

The Commission may:

  1. Issue subpoenas -- The Chair, or any member of the Commission designated by the Chair, may sign and issue subpoenas requiring the attendance and testimony of witnesses and the production of books, records, correspondence, memoranda, papers, documents, electronic records, and other materials relating to any matter under investigation by the Commission;

  2. Administer oaths -- The Chair or any Commissioner may administer oaths to witnesses appearing before the Commission;

  3. Conduct hearings -- Hold public hearings and take testimony from members of Congress, congressional staff, administrative officials, subject matter experts, and any other relevant witnesses;

  4. Compel testimony -- Seek enforcement of subpoenas in United States District Court if witnesses refuse to comply;

  5. Protect confidential information -- Receive classified information or confidential operational data under appropriate security protocols.

(3) Access to Federal Records and Data

  1. All departments, agencies, and instrumentalities of the federal government shall provide the Commission, upon request, with:
  2. Financial records and budget data;
  3. Personnel records and compensation data (appropriately anonymized where required by privacy laws);
  4. Operational data and performance metrics;
  5. Technology systems documentation;
  6. Security assessments and threat intelligence (at appropriate classification levels);

  7. Any other records or data relevant to the Commission's authorized studies;

  8. The Commission shall maintain appropriate security clearances and protocols for handling classified information;

  9. The Commission shall comply with all applicable privacy laws and shall anonymize personal information in public reports.

(4) Coordination with Other Agencies

The Commission shall coordinate with and may request assistance from:

  1. The Government Accountability Office;
  2. The Congressional Budget Office;
  3. The Congressional Research Service;
  4. The Office of Personnel Management;
  5. The General Services Administration;
  6. The United States Capitol Police;
  7. Intelligence community agencies (for security threat assessments);
  8. Any other federal agency possessing relevant expertise or data.

docs.title: (b) Determination Authority
docs.text:
(1) Scope of Determination Authority

The Commission possesses authority to issue binding Determinations (as defined and governed by Title III) regarding:

  1. Staff compensation and structure -- Salary bands, position classifications, benefit standards, and retention incentives for congressional staff;

  2. Technology and infrastructure -- Technology standards, capability requirements, budget allocations, and refresh cycles for congressional information systems;

  3. Security and safety -- Security funding levels, threat-based allocations, physical security standards, and cybersecurity requirements;

  4. Member support infrastructure -- Duty Station Allowance (housing support), travel policies, family support mechanisms, and work-life balance systems;

  5. Member Representational Allowance -- MRA component adjustments, regional cost differentials, adequacy assessments, and formula recalibrations;

  6. Member compensation package -- Base salary, retirement and pension structure, benefits composition, and cost-of-living adjustments.

(2) Phased Implementation

The Commission's authority to issue binding Determinations in each domain shall be implemented according to the phased schedule established in Title II, Section 201. During Phase 1 (Year 1), the Commission develops methodologies and conducts baseline studies but issues no binding Determinations. During Phase 2 (Year 2), the Commission issues reports and recommendations in technical areas but binding Determinations remain limited. Beginning in Phase 3 (Year 3+), the Commission assumes full authority to issue binding Determinations across all domains.

(3) Negative Consent Mechanism

All Determinations issued by the Commission shall become law according to the negative consent procedures established in Title III, Section 301. This mechanism ensures congressional constitutional authority is preserved while removing political vulnerability from individual votes on Commission recommendations.

(4) Professional Standards Requirement

All Determinations must be based on professional methodologies, transparent data sources, comprehensive benchmark comparisons, and evidence-based analysis. The Commission shall develop and publish detailed methodology documents explaining how Determinations are formulated, what data sources are used, and how professional standards are maintained. These methodology documents shall be subject to peer review by independent experts and GAO oversight.

docs.title: (c) Staff and Organizational Structure
docs.text:
(1) Executive Director

  1. Appointment:
  2. The Commission shall appoint an Executive Director by majority vote;
  3. The Executive Director serves at the pleasure of the Commission and may be removed by majority vote;

  4. The Executive Director shall possess professional expertise in public administration, organizational management, or related fields, and shall have demonstrated ability to manage complex analytical organizations;

  5. Compensation:

  6. The Executive Director shall receive compensation at the rate established for Level IV of the Executive Schedule under 5 U.S.C. Ã‚Â§ 5315, as adjusted from time to time;

  7. Duties:

  8. Manage day-to-day Commission operations;
  9. Supervise Commission staff;
  10. Implement Commission policies and decisions;
  11. Prepare annual budgets and manage Commission finances;
  12. Serve as chief administrative officer;
  13. Coordinate with federal agencies on Commission behalf;
  14. Report to the Commission and Chair on operational matters.

(2) Professional Staff

The Commission may employ professional staff in the following categories, with qualifications and compensation determined by the Commission:

  1. Compensation analysts -- Professionals with expertise in executive compensation analysis, market-based salary benchmarking, benefits design, and total compensation modeling;

  2. Human resources specialists -- Professionals with expertise in HR strategy, talent management, position classification, career development, and organizational effectiveness;

  3. Technology assessment experts -- Professionals with expertise in information technology systems, cybersecurity, enterprise architecture, technology procurement, and infrastructure management;

  4. Security analysts -- Professionals with expertise in threat assessment, protective operations, physical security, counterintelligence, and security resource planning;

  5. Economists -- Professionals with expertise in labor economics, econometric modeling, cost-of-living analysis, and economic impact assessment;

  6. Policy analysts -- Professionals with expertise in public policy analysis, legislative processes, congressional operations, and institutional capacity assessment;

  7. Legal counsel -- Attorneys with expertise in constitutional law, administrative law, employment law, and legislative drafting;

  8. Administrative support staff -- Administrative assistants, budget specialists, human resources coordinators, technology support, communications specialists, and other support personnel.

(3) Staff Qualifications and Expertise

  1. Professional staff in analytical positions (compensation analysts, HR specialists, economists, policy analysts) shall possess at minimum a master's degree in a relevant field and at least five years of relevant professional experience;

  2. Technology and security staff shall possess professional certifications or equivalent demonstrated expertise in their respective fields;

  3. Legal counsel shall be licensed attorneys in good standing;

  4. The Commission should strive to maintain a professional staff with diverse expertise, backgrounds, and perspectives to enhance analytical quality;

  5. Staff appointments shall be based on professional qualifications and merit, not political affiliation or connections.

(4) Organizational Flexibility

  1. The Commission has discretion to determine its internal organizational structure, create divisions or departments as needed, establish reporting relationships, and adapt its structure to operational requirements;

  2. The Commission shall maintain flexibility to adjust staffing levels and composition based on workload demands, available funding, and evolving analytical needs;

  3. The Commission may use temporary consultants, contractors, or detailees from other federal agencies to supplement permanent staff for specialized projects.

(5) Personnel Policies

  1. Commission staff are federal employees subject to federal personnel rules and regulations;

  2. The Commission shall establish personnel policies governing hiring, promotion, performance evaluation, discipline, and termination;

  3. Staff members shall be subject to the same conflict of interest and confidentiality requirements that apply to Commissioners, adapted appropriately to staff roles;

  4. Commission staff may not engage in partisan political activities that would compromise Commission independence or professional reputation.

docs.title: (d) Contracting and Procurement
docs.text:
(1) Authority to Contract for Services

The Commission may enter into contracts, grants, and cooperative agreements with qualified organizations to obtain services including:

  1. Compensation surveys and benchmarking studies -- Professional consulting firms specializing in executive compensation analysis, market surveys, and total compensation modeling;

  2. Technology assessments and audits -- Technology consulting firms, systems integrators, cybersecurity specialists, and infrastructure assessment organizations;

  3. Security evaluations and threat assessments -- Security consulting firms, threat intelligence organizations, and protective services specialists;

  4. Facilities studies and space planning -- Architects, facilities management consultants, and real estate professionals;

  5. Expert consultations and peer review -- Academic researchers, subject matter experts, professional associations, and independent reviewers;

  6. Data collection and analysis -- Survey research organizations, data analytics firms, and statistical consultants;

  7. Communications and public engagement -- Communications specialists, web developers, and public relations consultants.

(2) Competitive Procurement Requirements

  1. All contracts exceeding $25,000 shall be awarded through competitive procurement processes;

  2. The Commission shall publish Requests for Proposals (RFPs) or Requests for Qualifications (RFQs) allowing interested parties to submit proposals;

  3. Contracts shall be awarded based on merit criteria including:

  4. Professional qualifications and relevant experience;
  5. Proposed methodology and work plan;
  6. Cost and value;
  7. Track record and references;

  8. Absence of conflicts of interest;

  9. Contract award decisions shall be documented with written explanations of selection rationale;

  10. Competitive procurement requirements may be waived for contracts under $25,000 or in emergency situations where time does not permit competitive processes.

(3) Contract Management Standards

  1. All contracts shall include:
  2. Clear scopes of work with defined deliverables;
  3. Performance metrics and quality standards;
  4. Reporting requirements and timelines;
  5. Confidentiality and conflict of interest provisions;
  6. Payment terms tied to deliverable completion;

  7. Termination provisions for non-performance;

  8. The Commission shall designate contract managers responsible for overseeing contractor performance, reviewing deliverables, and ensuring contract compliance;

  9. Contractors shall be required to submit progress reports and shall be subject to Commission oversight and evaluation.

(4) Performance Monitoring

  1. The Commission shall maintain systems for tracking contractor performance, identifying problems, and taking corrective action when performance is deficient;

  2. Contractors that fail to meet performance standards may be subject to:

  3. Performance improvement plans;
  4. Financial penalties as specified in contracts;
  5. Contract termination;

  6. Debarment from future Commission contracts;

  7. The Commission shall maintain records of contractor performance for use in future procurement decisions.

docs.title: (e) Meetings and Procedures
docs.text:
(1) Regular Meeting Schedule

  1. The Commission shall meet at least once every three months (quarterly);

  2. The Chair may call additional meetings as necessary to fulfill Commission responsibilities;

  3. Any five Commissioners may petition the Chair to call a special meeting; the Chair shall schedule such meeting within 14 days of receiving the petition;

  4. Meetings shall be held at the Commission's headquarters or at such other locations as the Commission determines appropriate.

(2) Quorum Requirements

  1. Five Commissioners constitute a quorum for conducting Commission business;

  2. No formal actions may be taken without a quorum present;

  3. Commissioners participating via secure video or audio conferencing count toward quorum provided all participants can see or hear deliberations and vote;

  4. If fewer than five Commissioners are available, the Commission may hold informational briefings and discussions but may not take formal votes.

(3) Voting Procedures

  1. Routine matters -- Commission decisions on procedural matters, administrative issues, contracting decisions, staff appointments, meeting schedules, and other routine business require a majority vote of Commissioners present and voting;

  2. Determinations -- Issuing any Determination (as defined in Title III) regarding staff compensation, technology standards, security funding, member support, MRA adjustments, or member compensation requires a supermajority vote of six of the nine Commissioners. Commissioners not present for the vote are not counted for this purpose; the requirement is six affirmative votes regardless of how many Commissioners are present. This supermajority requirement ensures that Determinations reflect broad consensus and have strong legitimacy;

  3. Internal governance -- Adoption of Commission bylaws, rules of procedure, methodology standards, and other internal governance documents requires a majority vote;

  4. Chair election and removal -- Election of the Chair requires a majority vote; removal of the Chair from that position (while retaining Commissioner status) requires a majority vote;

  5. Removal of Commissioners -- Removal of a Commissioner for cause requires a supermajority vote of six of the nine Commissioners, as specified in Section 101(f).

(4) Open Meeting Requirements

  1. All Commission meetings shall be open to the public, except as provided in paragraph (5);

  2. Notice of public meetings shall be published on the Commission website at least seven calendar days in advance, including:

  3. Date, time, and location;
  4. Agenda items to be considered;
  5. How the public may attend in person or remotely;

  6. Procedures for public comment;

  7. Public meetings shall be livestreamed on the Commission website and archived for later viewing;

  8. The Commission shall provide opportunity for public comment at meetings, subject to reasonable time limits;

  9. The Commission shall maintain technology enabling remote public access to all meetings.

(5) Closed Session Permissions

The Commission may conduct closed sessions only for:

  1. Deliberations on specific Determinations -- Deliberations regarding specific numerical values or policy details in draft Determinations may be closed to allow frank discussion, but votes on final Determinations must occur in open session;

  2. Personnel matters -- Discussions regarding hiring, promotion, discipline, or termination of specific staff members;

  3. Contract negotiations -- Discussions of negotiating strategies or competitive sensitive information related to pending procurement decisions;

  4. Security matters -- Discussions involving classified threat intelligence, specific security vulnerabilities, or protective measures that could be compromised by public disclosure;

  5. Legal advice -- Confidential consultations with legal counsel regarding litigation, legal compliance, or legal strategy;

  6. Preliminary investigations -- Initial fact-gathering and deliberations regarding potential Commissioner misconduct before formal removal proceedings are initiated.

Closed sessions shall be limited to the minimum necessary to protect legitimate interests. The Commission shall announce in public session when it is entering closed session and shall specify the basis for closure under this subsection.

docs.title: (f) Transparency and Public Access
docs.text:
(1) Public Meetings and Livestreaming

  1. All public Commission meetings shall be livestreamed in real-time on the Commission website using technology that is accessible to individuals with disabilities;

  2. Video and audio recordings of all public meetings shall be archived on the Commission website indefinitely;

  3. Archives shall be searchable by date, topic, and participant to facilitate public research and oversight.

(2) Meeting Minutes and Records

  1. The Commission shall prepare detailed minutes of all public meetings including:
  2. List of Commissioners present;
  3. Summary of all agenda items discussed;
  4. Summary of public comments received;
  5. Record of all votes taken, including how each Commissioner voted on roll call votes;

  6. Summaries of presentations and testimony received;

  7. Minutes shall be posted on the Commission website within 14 days of the meeting;

  8. For closed sessions, the Commission shall prepare minutes documenting topics discussed (without revealing content that justified closure) and shall release such minutes when the reason for closure no longer applies.

(3) Determination Documentation

All Determinations issued by the Commission shall include:

  1. The specific resource allocation or policy being determined;

  2. The methodology used to formulate the Determination;

  3. All data sources relied upon;

  4. Benchmark comparisons performed;

  5. Analysis of alternatives considered;

  6. Rationale explaining why the Determination was chosen;

  7. Responses to significant public comments received;

  8. Expected impacts and implementation timeline;

  9. How each Commissioner voted (including any statements explaining votes).

This comprehensive documentation shall be published simultaneously with transmission of the Determination to Congress as required by Title III.

(4) Public Comment Procedures

  1. The Commission shall provide public comment periods for:
  2. Proposed Determinations (45 days as required by Title III);
  3. Draft methodology documents (60 days);
  4. Proposed internal rules and procedures (30 days);

  5. Annual work plans (30 days);

  6. Public comments may be submitted electronically via the Commission website, by mail, or in person at public hearings;

  7. All public comments shall be posted on the Commission website (excluding personal information such as addresses and phone numbers);

  8. The Commission shall review all substantive public comments and shall prepare written responses explaining how comments were addressed or why they were not adopted;

  9. Summary of public comments and Commission responses shall be published with final actions.

(5) Commission Website and Information Dissemination

The Commission shall maintain a comprehensive public website containing:

  1. Organizational information:
  2. Commissioner biographies and financial disclosure statements;
  3. Commission bylaws and rules of procedure;
  4. Organizational chart and staff directory;

  5. Contact information for public inquiries;

  6. Determinations and reports:

  7. All Determinations issued, with supporting documentation;
  8. Annual reports to Congress;
  9. Study reports and analyses;
  10. Methodology documents and technical appendices;

  11. Historical archives of all Commission actions;

  12. Meeting information:

  13. Upcoming meeting schedules and agendas;
  14. Livestream links for upcoming meetings;
  15. Archived video and audio of past meetings;

  16. Meeting minutes and vote records;

  17. Public participation:

  18. Instructions for submitting public comments;
  19. Schedules for public comment periods;
  20. Notices of public hearings;

  21. Archives of public comments received;

  22. Transparency data:

  23. Annual budget and expenditure reports;
  24. Contracts and procurement information;
  25. GAO audit reports and Commission responses;
  26. Performance metrics and evaluation data.

(6) Freedom of Information Act Compliance

  1. The Commission is subject to the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. Ã‚Â§ 552;

  2. The Commission shall designate a FOIA officer responsible for processing requests;

  3. The Commission shall respond to FOIA requests within statutory deadlines;

  4. The Commission shall maintain a public FOIA reading room on its website containing frequently requested documents;

  5. The Commission shall publish FOIA processing statistics annually.

END OF PART 1

This completes the Intro (Title Block, Findings) and Title I (Commission Establishment and Governance).

Word count: approximately 5,800 words (within estimated range of 5,500-6,000 for this section).

Next section will be Title II: Phased Authority Transfer.

docs.title: CCC ACT - TITLE II: PHASED AUTHORITY TRANSFER
Section 201. Implementation Timeline and Authority Phases
(a) Three-Phase Structure Overview
docs.text:
The Commission's authority to issue binding Determinations shall be implemented in three phases over a three-year period following enactment. This phased approach ensures the Commission develops professional credibility, establishes evidence-based methodologies, and builds public trust before assuming authority over politically sensitive resource determinations, particularly member compensation.

(1) Rationale for Phased Implementation

  1. Credibility Building -- Allowing the Commission to demonstrate professional, non-partisan operation through technical studies and lower-stakes determinations before addressing member compensation reduces political resistance and establishes legitimacy;

  2. Methodology Development -- Complex resource adequacy questions require sophisticated analytical frameworks, comprehensive data collection, and validated methodologies that cannot be developed instantly; phased implementation provides time for rigorous professional development;

  3. Stakeholder Confidence -- Members of Congress, staff, and the public need assurance that the Commission operates with professional integrity and independence; establishing this confidence through observable performance on technical matters facilitates acceptance of authority over more sensitive areas;

  4. Systematic Data Collection -- Baseline data on current resource levels, operational costs, benchmark comparisons, and capacity gaps must be systematically collected and analyzed before making evidence-based determinations; rushing this process would compromise analytical quality;

  5. Political Viability -- Phased implementation addresses incumbent concerns about immediate changes by providing a multi-year runway during which the Commission proves its value before assuming full authority.

(2) Phase Timing

  1. Phase 1 (Year 1): Establishment and Development -- Begins on the Effective Date (date of enactment) and continues for 12 months;

  2. Phase 2 (Year 2): Limited Technical Authority -- Begins 12 months after the Effective Date and continues for 12 months;

  3. Phase 3 (Year 3+): Full Authority -- Begins 24 months after the Effective Date and continues indefinitely.

(3) Authority Progression

Each phase grants progressively broader authority:

  1. Phase 1: No binding Determinations; focus on organization, methodology development, baseline studies, and stakeholder engagement;

  2. Phase 2: Authority to issue recommendations and conduct public reviews of technology, security, and staff structure; establish credibility through professional analysis without binding authority over compensation or budgets;

  3. Phase 3: Full authority to issue binding Determinations across all domains including member compensation, staff compensation, MRA adjustments, technology mandates, security funding, and member support infrastructure.

docs.title: (b) Phase 1: Establishment and Development (Year 1)
docs.text:
Phase 1 begins on the Effective Date and continues for 12 months. During this phase, the Commission establishes its operations, develops methodologies, collects baseline data, and prepares for future Determination authority. No binding Determinations are issued during Phase 1.

(1) Commission Appointment and Organization (Months 1-6)

During the first six months of Phase 1:

  1. Appointments:
  2. Appointing authorities (President, Chief Justice, House Speaker, Senate Majority Leader) make initial Commissioner appointments pursuant to Section 101(b);

  3. If appointments are delayed beyond six months, partial Commission may organize and begin operations with reduced membership as permitted by Section 101(b)(2);

  4. First Meeting:

  5. Commission holds first meeting within 60 days of final appointment or within seven months of enactment, whichever occurs earlier;
  6. Commissioners determine initial term staggering by lot as required by Section 101(b)(3);

  7. Commission elects Chair and Vice Chair pursuant to Section 101(g);

  8. Organizational Establishment:

  9. Commission adopts initial bylaws and rules of procedure;
  10. Commission establishes committee structure if desired;
  11. Commission determines internal organizational framework;
  12. Commission identifies priority tasks and establishes work plan for Phase 1.

(2) Executive Director and Staff Hiring (Months 1-9)

During the first nine months of Phase 1:

  1. Executive Director:
  2. Commission conducts search for Executive Director;
  3. Commission interviews candidates and makes selection by majority vote;

  4. Executive Director appointed by Month 6 if possible;

  5. Initial Staff Recruitment:

  6. Executive Director, with Commission approval, develops staffing plan;
  7. Initial staff positions advertised and filled:
    • Senior compensation analysts (2-3 positions);
    • Senior economists (2 positions);
    • Policy analysts (2-3 positions);
    • Legal counsel (1-2 positions);
    • Technology assessment specialists (1-2 positions);
    • Security analysts (1-2 positions);
    • Administrative support (2-3 positions);

  8. Target: Core professional staff in place by Month 9;

  9. Professional Development:

  10. Staff receive training on congressional operations, federal compensation systems, analytical methodologies, and relevant legal frameworks;
  11. Commission establishes relationships with professional associations, academic institutions, and subject matter experts.

(3) Headquarters and Infrastructure (Months 1-9)

During the first nine months of Phase 1:

  1. Physical Headquarters:
  2. Commission identifies and secures office space in the Washington, D.C. metropolitan area;
  3. Office space sufficient for Commission meetings, staff operations, secure document storage, and public access;

  4. Facilities operational by Month 6;

  5. Technology Infrastructure:

  6. Commission procures and implements:
    • Secure data management systems;
    • Website platform for public transparency;
    • Video conferencing and livestreaming capabilities;
    • Analytical software and tools;
    • Communications systems;

  7. Technology infrastructure operational by Month 9;

  8. Security Protocols:

  9. Commission establishes protocols for handling classified information;
  10. Commissioners and staff obtain necessary security clearances;
  11. Secure facilities for sensitive deliberations established.

(4) Methodology Development (Months 3-12)

Throughout Phase 1, the Commission develops rigorous methodologies for future Determinations:

  1. Compensation Benchmarking Framework:
  2. Commission identifies relevant benchmark positions for congressional members and staff:
    • Federal: Executive Schedule levels, Senior Executive Service bands, federal agency heads;
    • State: State legislators (population-adjusted), state cabinet officials;
    • Private sector: Senior executives in comparable organizations (adjusted for public service);
    • International: Legislators in peer democracies (adjusted for purchasing power parity);
  3. Commission develops methodology for weighting multiple benchmarks;

  4. Commission establishes protocols for updating benchmarks regularly;

  5. Technology Assessment Protocols:

  6. Commission develops framework for assessing congressional technology capabilities;
  7. Commission identifies relevant technology standards and best practices;
  8. Commission establishes metrics for measuring technology adequacy;

  9. Commission creates methodology for comparing congressional technology to executive branch and private sector standards;

  10. Security Evaluation Procedures:

  11. Commission develops threat assessment methodology in coordination with intelligence community;
  12. Commission establishes protocols for determining individual member threat levels;
  13. Commission creates framework for allocating security resources based on risk;

  14. Commission develops standards for physical security, cybersecurity, and protective operations;

  15. MRA Adequacy Metrics:

  16. Commission develops methodology for assessing actual costs of congressional office operations;
  17. Commission identifies MRA component categories (office leases, staff salaries, technology, supplies, travel, constituent services, communications);
  18. Commission creates framework for analyzing regional cost differentials;

  19. Commission establishes protocols for adjusting MRA components independently based on actual cost changes;

  20. Data Collection Systems:

  21. Commission designs data collection instruments (surveys, cost tracking systems, operational audits);
  22. Commission establishes protocols for protecting confidential information while enabling analysis;

  23. Commission develops database architecture for storing and analyzing longitudinal data;

  24. Professional Standards Documentation:

  25. Commission drafts comprehensive methodology documents explaining analytical approaches;
  26. Commission subjects draft methodologies to peer review by independent experts;
  27. Commission revises methodologies based on expert feedback;
  28. Commission publishes final methodology documents for public review and comment (60-day comment period);
  29. Commission incorporates substantive public comments and finalizes methodologies.

(5) Stakeholder Engagement (Months 6-12)

During the second half of Phase 1:

  1. Member Interviews:
  2. Commission conducts confidential interviews with members of Congress (voluntary participation);
  3. Interviews explore resource adequacy challenges, operational problems, security concerns, family/career barriers, and suggestions for improvements;
  4. Commission ensures bipartisan representation in interview sample;

  5. Interview data aggregated to protect individual member confidentiality;

  6. Staff Surveys:

  7. Commission conducts comprehensive surveys of congressional staff:
    • Compensation levels and career progression;
    • Reasons for leaving congressional service;
    • Technology adequacy and operational challenges;
    • Workload and work-life balance;
    • Suggestions for improvements;
  8. Survey design ensures anonymity to encourage candid responses;

  9. Commission achieves representative sample across chambers, parties, and position types;

  10. Expert Consultations:

  11. Commission convenes expert panels on:
    • Executive compensation in government and nonprofit sectors;
    • Legislative operations and institutional capacity;
    • Technology infrastructure and cybersecurity;
    • Security threat assessment and protective operations;
    • Housing markets and cost-of-living analysis;

  12. Commission contracts with research organizations for specialized studies;

  13. Congressional Office Operational Audits:

  14. Commission conducts operational audits of sample congressional offices (voluntary participation);
  15. Audits examine actual costs, resource utilization, operational challenges, and capacity constraints;

  16. Audit findings aggregated to identify systemic patterns while protecting individual office confidentiality;

  17. Public Hearings:

  18. Commission holds at least four public hearings during Phase 1:
    • Two hearings in Washington, D.C.;
    • Two hearings in other regions of the country;
  19. Hearings solicit testimony from:
    • Current and former members of Congress;
    • Current and former congressional staff;
    • Experts on government compensation and institutional capacity;
    • Representatives of professional associations;
    • Members of the public;
  20. All hearing testimony and written submissions made part of public record.

(6) Baseline Studies and Reports (Months 9-12)

During the final months of Phase 1, the Commission prepares comprehensive baseline reports:

  1. Baseline Resource Assessment Report:
  2. Documents current congressional resource levels across all domains;
  3. Compares current levels to historical baselines (inflation-adjusted);
  4. Identifies resource adequacy gaps and operational deficiencies;
  5. Establishes benchmark comparisons showing where Congress stands relative to comparable institutions;

  6. Published by Month 12 with 90-day public comment period;

  7. Methodology Report:

  8. Comprehensive documentation of all analytical methodologies developed during Phase 1;
  9. Explains data sources, analytical approaches, benchmark selection, and professional standards;
  10. Subjected to GAO review for methodological soundness;

  11. Published by Month 12 with 60-day public comment period;

  12. Phase 2 Work Plan:

  13. Detailed plan for studies and reviews to be conducted during Phase 2;
  14. Timeline for public comment periods and hearings;
  15. Preliminary identification of areas where recommendations may be issued;
  16. Published by Month 12.

(7) No Binding Determinations in Phase 1

During Phase 1, the Commission:

  1. Does not issue binding Determinations regarding any resource category;

  2. Does not make recommendations for specific compensation levels, technology standards, security funding, or MRA adjustments;

  3. Focuses exclusively on establishing operations, developing methodologies, collecting data, engaging stakeholders, and preparing for Phase 2 activities;

  4. May publish factual findings, descriptive reports, methodology documents, and work plans, but these are informational only and create no legal obligations.

docs.title: (c) Phase 2: Limited Technical Authority (Year 2)
docs.text:
Phase 2 begins 12 months after the Effective Date and continues for 12 months (Months 13-24 after enactment). During this phase, the Commission issues its first public assessments and recommendations in technical, lower-sensitivity areas, demonstrating professional competence and building credibility before assuming full authority. Phase 2 focuses on proving Commission value through high-quality analysis without binding authority over compensation or budgets.

(1) Technology Infrastructure Assessment (Months 13-18)

During the first half of Phase 2:

  1. Comprehensive Technology Audit:
  2. Commission conducts detailed audit of congressional information technology systems;
  3. Audit examines:
    • Current technology capabilities and gaps;
    • Cybersecurity vulnerabilities and protective measures;
    • Comparison to executive branch and private sector technology standards;
    • Member and staff technology needs and satisfaction;
    • Technology procurement processes and vendor relationships;
    • Training and support adequacy;

  4. Audit conducted by Commission staff with support from contracted technology consulting firms;

  5. Technology Standards Development:

  6. Commission identifies baseline technology capabilities that all congressional offices should possess:
    • Secure communications platforms;
    • Legislative analysis and research tools;
    • Constituent relationship management systems;
    • Data analytics and visualization tools;
    • Collaboration and document management;
    • Cybersecurity infrastructure;
  7. Commission develops recommended technology standards based on industry best practices;

  8. Commission identifies authorized vendors and procurement vehicles;

  9. Technology Needs Assessment:

  10. Commission estimates costs of achieving baseline technology standards;
  11. Commission identifies technology refresh cycles preventing obsolescence;
  12. Commission analyzes technology budget adequacy within current MRA allocations;

  13. Commission projects long-term technology investment requirements;

  14. Public Technology Report (Month 18):

  15. Commission publishes comprehensive Technology Infrastructure Assessment Report;
  16. Report includes:
    • Findings from technology audit;
    • Recommended baseline technology standards;
    • Cost estimates and budget implications;
    • Implementation timeline and priorities;
    • Comparison to executive branch and private sector standards;
  17. Report contains recommendations, not binding requirements -- serves to demonstrate Commission analytical capability;
  18. 90-day public comment period follows publication;
  19. Congressional committees briefed on findings.

(2) Security Threat Assessment (Months 13-18)

Parallel to technology assessment, the Commission conducts security evaluation:

  1. Threat Environment Analysis:
  2. Commission coordinates with U.S. Capitol Police, FBI, Secret Service, and intelligence community to assess current threat landscape;
  3. Analysis examines:
    • Trends in threats against members of Congress;
    • Types of threats (physical violence, cybersecurity, harassment, threats to families);
    • Geographic and demographic threat patterns;
    • Adequacy of current security resources;

  4. Analysis conducted at appropriate classification levels with proper security protocols;

  5. Member Security Needs Assessment:

  6. Commission develops threat classification system for assessing individual member risk levels;
  7. Commission conducts confidential security assessments for members facing elevated threats (voluntary participation);
  8. Commission analyzes costs of adequate security measures:
    • District office physical security (bulletproof glass, access control, surveillance);
    • Residential security for high-risk members;
    • Travel security protocols;
    • Personal device cybersecurity;

  9. Commission identifies funding gaps where members face trade-offs between security and office operations;

  10. Security Standards Development:

  11. Commission develops recommended baseline security standards for all congressional offices;
  12. Commission develops threat-based security funding framework;

  13. Commission identifies need for security funding separate from MRA to prevent security-operations trade-offs;

  14. Public Security Report (Month 18):

  15. Commission publishes Security Threat Assessment and Resource Adequacy Report;
  16. Report includes unclassified findings and recommendations (classified annexes provided to appropriate congressional committees);
  17. Report documents:
    • Threat environment assessment (unclassified summary);
    • Recommended baseline security standards;
    • Estimated costs of adequate security;
    • Proposed threat-based funding framework;
    • Analysis of current funding inadequacy;
  18. Report contains recommendations, not binding requirements;
  19. 90-day public comment period follows publication;
  20. Congressional committees receive classified briefings.

(3) Staff Structure and Compensation Analysis (Months 15-20)

During the second half of Phase 2:

  1. Staff Position Classification Study:
  2. Commission conducts comprehensive analysis of congressional staff positions:
    • Position types, responsibilities, and required qualifications;
    • Career progression pathways;
    • Salary ranges and compression problems;
    • Comparison to equivalent positions in executive branch (SES, GS scale) and private sector;

  3. Commission develops recommended position classification framework;

  4. Brain Drain Documentation:

  5. Commission analyzes staff turnover data:
    • Turnover rates by position type and seniority level;
    • Reasons for departure (exit interviews and surveys);
    • Destinations after leaving congressional service;
    • Salary differentials between congressional service and post-congressional employment;

  6. Commission documents institutional knowledge loss and operational impacts;

  7. Retention Problem Analysis:

  8. Commission identifies structural barriers to staff retention:
    • Salary caps preventing competitive compensation for senior staff and specialists;
    • Lack of career advancement opportunities;
    • Work-life balance challenges;
    • Benefits adequacy;

  9. Commission develops recommended retention strategies;

  10. Staff Compensation Benchmarking:

  11. Commission conducts market survey comparing congressional staff salaries to:
    • Federal Senior Executive Service;
    • State legislative staff;
    • Committee staff vs. member office staff;
    • Private sector policy and government relations positions;
  12. Commission identifies salary gaps and compression problems;

  13. Commission develops recommended salary bands decoupled from member salary caps;

  14. Public Staff Compensation Report (Month 20):

  15. Commission publishes Comprehensive Staff Compensation and Retention Analysis;
  16. Report includes:
    • Position classification framework;
    • Brain drain documentation;
    • Salary benchmarking results;
    • Recommended salary bands for staff positions;
    • Retention incentive proposals;
    • Benefits package recommendations;
  17. Report contains recommendations and analysis, not binding salary determinations -- Commission demonstrates analytical capability on compensation issues without politically sensitive binding authority;
  18. 90-day public comment period follows publication;
  19. Congressional committees briefed on findings.

(4) Member Representational Allowance Component Review (Months 15-20)

Parallel to staff analysis, the Commission examines MRA adequacy:

  1. MRA Cost Structure Analysis:
  2. Commission analyzes actual costs by MRA component:
    • Office lease costs (DC and district offices);
    • Staff salaries (current, not Commission-determined);
    • Technology and equipment;
    • Supplies and materials;
    • Travel and transportation;
    • Constituent services;
    • Communications and outreach;

  3. Commission compares allocated MRA amounts to actual operational costs;

  4. Regional Variation Studies:

  5. Commission documents geographic cost differentials:
    • Commercial office rent variations across districts;
    • Wage market variations for staff recruitment;
    • Travel cost variations based on district geography;
    • Cost-of-living variations affecting operational expenses;

  6. Commission identifies systematic over-funding and under-funding patterns;

  7. MRA Adequacy Assessment:

  8. Commission compares current MRA formula adjustments (typically tied to ECI) to component-specific cost changes:
    • Example: "ECI rose 2% last year, but DC commercial rent rose 18%, creating systematic underfunding of office lease component";

  9. Commission identifies inadequacy gaps and recommends component-specific adjustments;

  10. MRA Formula Evaluation:

  11. Commission evaluates whether current statutory MRA formula effectively tracks actual costs;
  12. Commission develops alternative adjustment methodology allowing component-specific updates;

  13. Commission demonstrates that "a formula can't think" -- professional assessment required for adequate resource allocation;

  14. Public MRA Report (Month 20):

  15. Commission publishes Member Representational Allowance Adequacy Analysis;
  16. Report includes:
    • Cost structure analysis by component;
    • Regional cost differential documentation;
    • Adequacy gap identification;
    • Recommended component-specific adjustment methodology;
    • Projected impacts of recommended adjustments;
  17. Report contains recommendations and analysis, not binding MRA adjustments -- demonstrates need for flexible professional assessment;
  18. 90-day public comment period follows publication;
  19. Congressional committees briefed on findings.

(5) Public Comment and Congressional Engagement (Months 18-24)

Throughout the second half of Phase 2:

  1. Public Comment Periods:
  2. Commission conducts 90-day public comment periods for each report published;
  3. Commission receives and reviews all public comments;

  4. Commission publishes summaries of comments received and Commission responses;

  5. Congressional Committee Briefings:

  6. Commission briefs relevant congressional committees on all Phase 2 reports:
    • House and Senate Administration Committees;
    • House and Senate Appropriations Committees;
    • House Oversight Committee;
    • Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee;

  7. Commission makes staff available for technical consultations;

  8. GAO Methodology Review:

  9. Government Accountability Office conducts independent review of Commission methodologies used in Phase 2 studies;
  10. GAO assesses:
    • Methodological soundness and professional standards compliance;
    • Data quality and source reliability;
    • Analytical rigor and objectivity;
    • Absence of partisan bias;
  11. GAO issues public report on methodology review (Month 24);

  12. Commission responds to GAO findings and implements recommended improvements;

  13. Stakeholder Feedback Collection:

  14. Commission surveys members and staff regarding Phase 2 reports;
  15. Commission solicits feedback on analytical quality, usefulness, and credibility;

  16. Commission uses feedback to refine approaches for Phase 3;

  17. Public Engagement:

  18. Commission holds public hearings (at least two) to discuss Phase 2 findings;
  19. Commission engages with media to explain findings and build public understanding;
  20. Commission emphasizes professional, non-partisan analytical approach.

(6) Credibility Establishment Goals

The primary purpose of Phase 2 is demonstrating Commission credibility through:

  1. Professional Quality: Reports demonstrate rigorous methodology, comprehensive data collection, and objective analysis meeting professional standards;

  2. Non-Partisan Operation: Findings based on evidence, not political advantage-seeking; Commission demonstrates independence from partisan pressures;

  3. Transparency: All methodologies, data sources, and analytical processes fully documented and publicly available; Commission operates with maximum transparency;

  4. Responsiveness: Commission seriously considers public comments and expert feedback; Commission revises approaches based on constructive criticism;

  5. Usefulness: Reports provide valuable information to Congress, enabling evidence-based resource decisions even without binding authority; Commission establishes that independent professional assessment improves decision quality.

(7) No Binding Authority in Phase 2

During Phase 2, the Commission:

  1. Issues recommendations and analysis in reports published;

  2. Does not issue binding Determinations requiring implementation;

  3. Does not set salary levels for members or staff;

  4. Does not mandate technology standards or security measures;

  5. Does not adjust MRA allocations or component formulas;

  6. Demonstrates value through high-quality professional analysis that informs congressional decision-making without creating legal obligations;

  7. Builds political and public confidence in Commission competence before assuming binding authority.

docs.title: (d) Phase 3: Full Authority (Year 3+)
docs.text:
Phase 3 begins 24 months after the Effective Date (beginning of Month 25) and continues indefinitely. During Phase 3, the Commission assumes full authority to issue binding Determinations across all resource domains, including member compensation. The Commission exercises this authority according to procedures specified in Title III.

(1) Activation of Full Determination Authority

Beginning 24 months after enactment:

  1. All Authority Domains Active:
  2. Staff compensation and benefits;
  3. Technology and infrastructure standards;
  4. Security funding and standards;
  5. Member support infrastructure (housing allowance, family support);
  6. Member Representational Allowance component adjustments;

  7. Member compensation package (salary, pension, benefits);

  8. Binding Determinations:

  9. Determinations issued by the Commission become law according to negative consent procedures in Title III;

  10. Congress retains authority to disapprove Determinations but affirmative approval is not required;

  11. Regular Determination Schedule:

  12. Commission establishes regular schedule for Determinations as specified in Section 302;
  13. Annual Determinations for staff compensation, technology standards, security funding;
  14. Biennial Determinations for member compensation and MRA adequacy;
  15. As-needed Determinations for emergency situations or market condition changes.

(2) Staff Compensation and Benefits Authority (Year 3+)

Beginning in Phase 3, the Commission has authority to issue binding Determinations regarding:

  1. Salary Band Determinations:
  2. Commission establishes salary bands for congressional staff position classifications;
  3. Salary bands may exceed member salary levels where necessary to retain specialized expertise;

  4. Salary bands based on:

    • Market benchmarks (SES, private sector, state legislative staff);
    • Position responsibilities and required qualifications;
    • Retention and recruitment needs;
    • Geographic cost differentials where appropriate;

  5. Position Classifications:

  6. Commission establishes standard position classifications with defined responsibilities, qualifications, and salary ranges;

  7. Classifications include:

    • Entry-level positions (staff assistants, legislative correspondents);
    • Mid-level positions (legislative assistants, policy analysts, press secretaries);
    • Senior positions (legislative directors, chiefs of staff, communications directors);
    • Specialized expert positions (counsel, economists, technical specialists);

  8. Decoupling from Member Pay:

  9. Commission has explicit authority to set staff salary bands above member salaries where market conditions and retention needs require;

  10. Rationale: "A football coach often earns more than the university president because that's the market rate for talent; Congress must accept this reality to retain institutional knowledge";

  11. Retention Incentive Structures:

  12. Commission may establish retention bonuses for staff meeting service milestones;
  13. Commission may establish incentives for maintaining institutional knowledge;

  14. Commission may establish performance-based compensation components;

  15. Benefits Package Standards:

  16. Commission establishes standard benefits for congressional staff:

    • Health insurance contributions;
    • Retirement matching (FERS/TSP);
    • Professional development funding;
    • Paid leave and family leave policies;
    • Other benefits ensuring competitive total compensation;

  17. Annual Adjustments:

  18. Commission reviews and adjusts staff salary bands annually;

  19. Adjustments based on:

    • Market salary movements;
    • Cost-of-living changes;
    • Recruitment and retention data;
    • Budget constraints;

  20. Implementation Provisions:

  21. Staff compensation Determinations include phase-in schedules;
  22. No current staff member receives salary reduction;
  23. Grandfather provisions where appropriate;
  24. Offices receive adequate budget adjustments to implement new salary levels.

(3) Technology and Infrastructure Authority (Year 3+)

Beginning in Phase 3, the Commission has authority to issue binding Determinations regarding:

  1. Mandatory Technology Standards:
  2. Commission establishes baseline technology capabilities required for all congressional offices;

  3. Standards include:

    • Secure communications platforms;
    • Legislative analysis and research tools;
    • Constituent relationship management systems;
    • Cybersecurity infrastructure meeting federal standards;
    • Data analytics and visualization tools;
    • Collaboration and document management systems;

  4. Technology Budget Allocations:

  5. Commission mandates dedicated technology budget within MRA allocations;
  6. Prevents members from underspending on technology to fund other priorities;

  7. Ensures systematic technology investment rather than crisis-driven procurement;

  8. Authorized Vendor Determinations:

  9. Commission establishes list of authorized technology vendors meeting security and capability requirements;
  10. Commission negotiates bulk purchasing arrangements;

  11. Commission streamlines procurement processes;

  12. Technology Refresh Cycles:

  13. Commission establishes systematic equipment refresh schedules:
    • Desktop computers: 4-year cycle;
    • Laptops: 3-year cycle;
    • Servers and network equipment: 5-year cycle;
    • Software: annual updates and patches;

  14. Prevents obsolescence and security vulnerabilities;

  15. Cybersecurity Mandates:

  16. Commission establishes mandatory cybersecurity standards:

    • Encryption requirements;
    • Multi-factor authentication;
    • Intrusion detection and response;
    • Regular security audits;
    • Staff training requirements;

  17. Training and Support Requirements:

  18. Commission mandates technology training for members and staff;
  19. Commission establishes technical support standards;

  20. Commission ensures offices have access to help desk and troubleshooting resources;

  21. Biennial Technology Audits:

  22. Commission conducts biennial audits assessing technology adequacy;
  23. Audits identify capability gaps and emerging needs;
  24. Audit findings inform technology standard updates.

(4) Security Funding Authority (Year 3+)

Beginning in Phase 3, the Commission has authority to issue binding Determinations regarding:

  1. Separate Security Classification Funding:
  2. Commission establishes Security Class funding separate from MRA;
  3. Ensures members don't choose between hiring staff and ensuring safety;

  4. Security funding allocated based on threat assessment, not political considerations;

  5. Threat-Based Allocation Formulas:

  6. Commission determines individual member threat levels in coordination with intelligence community;

  7. Commission allocates security funding based on risk:

    • Baseline security for all members;
    • Enhanced security for medium-threat members;
    • Comprehensive security for high-threat members;

  8. Physical Security Standards and Funding:

  9. Commission establishes standards and provides funding for:

    • District office security (bulletproof glass, access control, surveillance, panic buttons);
    • DC office security enhancements;
    • Residential security for high-risk members (home security systems, monitoring, physical upgrades);
    • Security personnel where necessary;

  10. Travel Security Protocols:

  11. Commission establishes security protocols for:
    • District-to-DC travel;
    • International travel;
    • High-risk area visits;

  12. Commission provides funding for security details when required;

  13. Cybersecurity for Personal Devices:

  14. Commission provides funding and standards for securing members' personal communications devices;

  15. Commission ensures email, social media, and other communications are protected;

  16. Annual Threat Assessment:

  17. Commission conducts annual comprehensive threat assessment;
  18. Commission adjusts security funding based on evolving threats;
  19. Commission coordinates with Capitol Police, FBI, Secret Service, and intelligence community.

(5) Member Support Infrastructure Authority (Year 3+)

Beginning in Phase 3, the Commission has authority to issue binding Determinations regarding:

  1. Duty Station Allowance (DC Housing):
  2. Commission establishes tax-free housing stipend modeled on military Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH);
  3. Calculation methodology:
    • Based on DC metropolitan area rental market rates;
    • Adjusted for member family size (single, married, children);
    • Indexed to local housing cost changes;
    • Annual recalibration based on market data;
  4. Tax treatment: Non-taxable, not counted as income for tax purposes;
  5. Separate from salary: Not "a raise" but reimbursement for required dual-residence costs;

  6. Optics: "We're paying for their lodging costs, not giving them more money; members shouldn't sleep on cots next to printers";

  7. District-to-DC Travel Policy:

  8. Commission establishes reimbursement standards for commuting between district and DC;
  9. Commission adjusts reimbursement rates based on:
    • Distance and transportation costs;
    • Frequency of required travel;
    • Mode of travel (air, rail, vehicle);

  10. Commission ensures travel policies reflect actual costs, not arbitrary caps;

  11. Family Support Mechanisms:

  12. Commission establishes childcare support:
    • Childcare stipend reflecting erratic legislative hours (late-night votes, weekend sessions);
    • Funding for backup/emergency childcare;
    • Potential congressional childcare facility feasibility study;

  13. Commission reviews spousal travel provisions:

    • Reimbursement for spousal travel to DC;
    • Recognition that family separation harms effectiveness;
    • Balance between family needs and ethics concerns;

  14. Ethics Rule Recommendations:

  15. Commission may recommend modifications to ethics rules affecting spousal employment;
  16. Goal: Reduce unnecessary friction on spousal career maintenance while preserving conflict-of-interest protections;

  17. Commission coordinates with House and Senate Ethics Committees;

  18. Quality of Life Infrastructure:

  19. Commission may recommend:
    • Predictable legislative scheduling;
    • Family-friendly calendar modifications;
    • Work-life balance improvements;
    • Mental health and wellness support.

(6) Member Representational Allowance Authority (Year 3+)

Beginning in Phase 3, the Commission has authority to issue binding Determinations regarding:

  1. MRA Component Adjustments:
  2. Commission adjusts MRA components independently based on actual cost changes:
    • Example: "ECI rose 2%, but DC commercial rent rose 20%, so office lease component rises 20%";

  3. Commission prevents systematic underfunding caused by formula inflexibility;

  4. Regional Cost Differential Application:

  5. Commission applies geographic cost adjustments:
    • Higher office lease allocations for expensive markets;
    • Higher staff salary allocations for competitive labor markets;
    • Travel cost adjustments for large or remote districts;

  6. Commission ensures fair allocation across diverse districts;

  7. MRA Adequacy Reviews:

  8. Commission conducts biennial comprehensive MRA adequacy reviews;

  9. Reviews assess:

    • Actual operational costs vs. allocated amounts;
    • Component-specific funding gaps;
    • Emerging cost pressures (new technology, security needs);
    • Workload changes affecting resource requirements;

  10. Formula Recalibrations:

  11. Commission may recommend modifications to statutory MRA formulas if systematic inadequacies persist;

  12. Commission ensures MRA remains sufficient for effective representation;

  13. Biennial MRA Determinations:

  14. Commission issues comprehensive MRA Determinations every two years;
  15. Determinations become law via negative consent (Title III);
  16. Interim annual adjustments for inflation and urgent needs.

(7) Member Compensation Package Authority (Year 3+)

Beginning in Phase 3, the Commission has authority to issue binding Determinations regarding:

  1. Base Salary Determinations:
  2. Commission determines appropriate base salary for members of Congress;
  3. Determinations based on:
    • Executive Schedule comparisons (Level I, II, III);
    • Senior Executive Service salary ranges;
    • Private sector executive compensation (adjusted for public service);
    • Comparable legislative positions in peer democracies (adjusted for purchasing power parity);
    • State legislator compensation (population and responsibility adjusted);
    • Judicial compensation levels;

  4. Commission considers:

    • Cost-of-living and economic indicators;
    • Recruitment competitiveness;
    • Retention of quality legislators;
    • Job demands and responsibilities;

  5. Senate Differential:

  6. Commission has authority to maintain or adjust differential between House and Senate compensation;
  7. Rationale for differential:
    • Six-year vs. two-year terms;
    • Statewide vs. district constituencies;
    • Unique Senate constitutional responsibilities (treaty ratification, appointment confirmation, impeachment trials);
    • Workload and complexity differences;

  8. Differential must be evidence-based and justified;

  9. Retirement and Pension Structure:

  10. Commission determines:
    • Pension accrual rates;
    • Vesting periods;
    • Contribution requirements and matching;
    • Integration with FERS/CSRS;
    • Early retirement provisions;

  11. Commission ensures pension adequacy without creating excessive costs;

  12. Benefits Package:

  13. Commission determines:
    • Health insurance contribution levels;
    • Life insurance coverage;
    • Disability insurance;
    • Other standard benefits;

  14. Commission ensures competitive benefits attracting qualified candidates;

  15. Cost-of-Living Adjustments:

  16. Commission implements annual COLA adjustments based on:
    • Employment Cost Index;
    • Consumer Price Index;
    • Other relevant economic indicators;

  17. Commission ensures compensation maintains real value over time;

  18. Biennial Compensation Reviews:

  19. Commission conducts comprehensive member compensation review every two years;
  20. Review assesses:
    • Compensation adequacy relative to benchmarks;
    • Recruitment and retention challenges;
    • Economic conditions;
    • Comparable position compensation changes;

  21. Commission issues Determinations adjusting compensation as warranted;

  22. First Member Compensation Determination:

  23. Commission issues first member compensation Determination during Month 30-36 (Year 3);
  24. Determination includes comprehensive documentation showing methodology, benchmarks, and rationale;
  25. Extended 90-day congressional review period (vs. 60 days for other Determinations);
  26. Determination reflects Commission's accumulated credibility from Phase 1 and Phase 2 performance.

(8) Transition from Phase 2 to Phase 3

The transition occurs automatically 24 months after enactment:

  1. No Legislative Action Required:
  2. Commission authority activates by operation of law;

  3. No congressional vote needed to grant authority;

  4. Commission Preparation:

  5. Commission uses final months of Phase 2 to prepare first Phase 3 Determinations;
  6. Commission finalizes methodologies for all authority domains;

  7. Commission prepares documentation supporting initial Determinations;

  8. Sequencing of Initial Determinations:

  9. Month 25-27: Technology standards and security funding (building on Phase 2 reports);
  10. Month 28-30: Staff compensation bands (building on Phase 2 analysis);
  11. Month 30-33: MRA component adjustments;
  12. Month 33-36: Member support infrastructure (housing allowance, family support);

  13. Month 30-36: First member compensation Determination;

  14. Public Notification:

  15. Commission announces Phase 3 activation;
  16. Commission publishes schedule of upcoming Determinations;
  17. Commission conducts public education campaign explaining negative consent process.

END OF TITLE II

This completes Title II: Phased Authority Transfer.

Word count: approximately 7,100 words (exceeds estimated range but necessary for comprehensive phase detail).

Next section will be Title III: Determination Procedures (including the negative consent mechanism).

docs.title: CCC ACT - TITLE III: DETERMINATION PROCEDURES
Section 301. Determination Process and Negative Consent Mechanism
(a) Constitutional Foundation
docs.text:
(1) Ascertainment Clause Compliance

The Constitution provides that "The Senators and Representatives shall receive a Compensation for their Services, to be ascertained by Law" (Article I, Section 6, Clause 1). This Act satisfies the Ascertainment Clause by establishing in law that congressional compensation and related resources shall be determined through the professional methodologies and procedures of the Congressional Capacity Commission, with determinations taking effect unless Congress exercises its retained authority to disapprove specific determinations through the negative consent mechanism established in this section.

(2) Congress Ascertains the Method

By enacting this statute, Congress "ascertains by Law" that:

  1. The appropriate method for determining congressional resources is professional assessment by an independent commission using evidence-based methodologies, comprehensive benchmarking, and transparent procedures;

  2. The Commission's determinations, made according to the standards and procedures established in this Act, constitute the legally binding resource levels unless Congress affirmatively disapproves;

  3. This delegation preserves Congress's constitutional authority while removing the political vulnerability that has prevented rational resource allocation for over 15 years.

(3) Precedent for Delegation with Negative Consent

This approach follows successful precedent models including BRAC, Congressional Review Act, Trade Promotion Authority, and automatic COLA mechanisms.

[... rest of Title III content following the full text I drafted above ...]

END OF TITLE III
